raw_results.question: What are the two pre-training objectives used in BERT?
raw_results.ground_truth: BERT uses masked language modeling (MLM) and next sentence prediction (NSP) as its two pre-training objectives.
raw_results.answer: BERT uses two pre-training objectives: masked language modeling (MLM) and next sentence prediction (NSP). [Source: RoBERTa.pdf | Section: 7 Conclusion | p.10] MLM involves selecting a random sample of tokens in the input sequence and replacing them with the special token [MASK], then predicting the masked tokens using a cross-entropy loss. NSP aims to predict whether a given pair of sentences forms a coherent paragraph, where the first sentence is taken from the same document and the second from another document. [Source: RoBERTa.pdf | Section: 2.3 Training Objectives | p.2]

raw_results.question: What is the key difference between LoRA and full fine-tuning?
raw_results.ground_truth: LoRA freezes the pretrained model weights and injects trainable low-rank decomposition matrices into each layer, significantly reducing the number of trainable parameters compared to full fine-tuning.
raw_results.answer: As reported in the provided documents, LoRA saves much trainable parameters and memory cost compared to full fine-tuning [Source: LongLoRA.pdf | Section: 3.3 IMPROVED LORA FOR LONG CONTEXT | p.5]. Empirically, the authors observe an obvious gap between LoRA and full fine-tuning as the target context length increases [Source: LongLoRA.pdf | Section: 3.3 IMPROVED LORA FOR LONG CONTEXT | p.5]. At the time of publication, LoRA with larger ranks could not reduce this gap [Source: LongLoRA.pdf | Section: 3.3 IMPROVED LORA FOR LONG CONTEXT | p.5].

raw_results.question: How does FlashAttention reduce memory usage compared to standard attention?
raw_results.ground_truth: FlashAttention uses tiling and recomputation to avoid materializing the full attention matrix in GPU HBM, reducing memory from O(N^2) to O(N) while maintaining exact attention computation.
raw_results.answer: FlashAttention reduces memory usage linearly instead of quadratically, compared to standard attention, which increases memory usage quadratically with sequence length [Source: FlashAttention_2.pdf | Section: 2 Background | p.2]. This is achieved by exploiting the asymmetric GPU memory hierarchy and reducing communication and shared memory reads/writes [Source: FlashAttention_2.pdf | Section: Faster Attention with Better Parallelism and Work Partitioning | p.1]. Specifically, benchmarks show that FlashAttention-2 achieves around 2├Ч speedup over FlashAttention, reaching up to 73% of the theoretical maximum throughput in the forward pass, indicating a significant reduction in memory usage [Source: FlashAttention_2.pdf | Section: Faster Attention with Better Parallelism and Work Partitioning | p.1].

raw_results.question: What is the main contribution of the RAG paper by Lewis et al.?
raw_results.ground_truth: RAG combines parametric memory (a pre-trained seq2seq model) with non-parametric memory (a dense vector index of Wikipedia) for knowledge-intensive NLP tasks.
raw_results.answer: The main contribution of the RAG paper by Lewis et al. (2020) is to enhance the input questions of generative language models with retrieved documents, thereby improving their performance in various tasks, especially in knowledge-intensive domains [Source: RAG_Lewis_et_al.pdf | Section: Acknowledgments | p.10 | score: 0.711]. At the time of publication, it was noted that RAG enhances factualness and control in generated responses [Source: RAG_Lewis_et_al.pdf | Section: Acknowledgments | p.10 | score: 0.711]. However, the exact quantitative improvements or benchmarks achieved by RAG are not reported in the provided documents.

raw_results.question: How does Self-RAG differ from standard RAG in terms of retrieval strategy?
raw_results.ground_truth: Self-RAG trains the model to adaptively retrieve passages on-demand by generating special reflection tokens, rather than always retrieving at every generation step.
raw_results.answer: Self-RAG differs from standard RAG in that it evaluates the necessity of retrieval based on different scenarios, whereas standard RAG likely employs a fixed retrieval process. This flexibility allows Self-RAG to more easily integrate with other technologies such as fine-tuning or reinforcement learning, as discussed in Section 5.7 of the document [Source: Corrective_RAG.pdf | Section: 5.7 Consistent Supplementation of Web Search Knowledge | p.9].

raw_results.question: What problem does GraphRAG solve that naive RAG cannot handle well?
raw_results.ground_truth: GraphRAG addresses global queries that require synthesizing information across an entire corpus by building a knowledge graph and using community summaries for query-focused summarization.
raw_results.answer: GraphRAG addresses the limitation of naive RAG in handling iterative question answering, which characterizes sensemaking activity. While naive RAG may struggle with the complexity and depth required for continuous querying and summarization, GraphRAG offers a highly efficient method that retains advantages in comprehensiveness (72% win rate) and diversity (62% win rate) over vector RAG [Source: GraphRAG.pdf | Section: 2.2 Using Knowledge Graphs with LLMs and RAG | p.3]. GraphRAG's ability to generate and iteratively aggregate global summaries, combined with its use of hierarchical indexing, allows it to provide more comprehensive and diverse responses compared to naive RAG [Source: GraphRAG.pdf | Section: 2.2 Using Knowledge Graphs with LLMs and RAG | p.3].

raw_results.question: How does Diffusion Policy represent robot action distributions?
raw_results.ground_truth: Diffusion Policy represents robot actions as a conditional denoising diffusion process, modeling the action distribution as the output of a denoising diffusion probabilistic model conditioned on observations.
raw_results.answer: Diffusion Policy represents robot action distributions by learning the gradient of the action-score function and iteratively optimizing with respect to this gradient field during inference via a series of stochastic Langevin dynamics steps [Source: Diffusion_Policy.pdf | Section: Learning via Action Diffusion | p.1 | score: 0.881]. This approach allows robot policies to inherit key properties from diffusion models, significantly improving performance and enabling the expression of multimodal action distributions [Source: Diffusion_Policy.pdf | Section: Learning via Action Diffusion | p.1 | score: 0.881].

raw_results.question: How does CLIP align visual and textual representations?
raw_results.ground_truth: CLIP trains an image encoder and text encoder jointly using a contrastive loss on a large dataset of image-text pairs from the internet, maximizing similarity for matching pairs and minimizing it for non-matching pairs.
raw_results.answer: CLIP learns robust visual-textual representations by leveraging contrastive learning techniques [Source: CLIP_Adapter.pdf | Section: 2 Related Work | p.3]. Specifically, CLIP has been shown to learn representations that capture both semantics and style of images [Source: CLIP_Adapter.pdf | Section: 2 Related Work | p.3]. This allows CLIP to generate image embeddings conditioned on text captions, enabling a two-stage hierarchical text-conditioned image generation model [Source: DALL-E_2.pdf | Section: Image Generation with CLIP Latents | p.1]. The model explicitly generates image representations, which improves image diversity with minimal loss in photorealism and caption similarity [Source: DALL-E_2.pdf | Section: Image Generation with CLIP Latents | p.1].

raw_results.question: What is the chain-of-thought prompting technique?
raw_results.ground_truth: Chain-of-thought prompting elicits multi-step reasoning in large language models by providing few-shot examples that include intermediate reasoning steps before the final answer.
raw_results.answer: Chain-of-thought prompting is a technique that guides large language models to produce a sequence of intermediate reasoning steps before providing the final answer. This method is designed to enhance the complex reasoning capabilities of language models. It involves providing a few examples of chains of thought as exemplars in the prompting process. The technique has been shown to significantly improve performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks. At the time of the publication of the provided documents, chain-of-thought prompting had achieved state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks like GSM8K [Source: Chain-of-Thought_Prompting.pdf | Section: Abstract | p.1].

raw_results.question: How does DeepSeek-R1 train reasoning capabilities without supervised chain-of-thought data?
raw_results.ground_truth: DeepSeek-R1 uses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) reinforcement learning with accuracy and format rewards to develop reasoning capabilities from scratch, then applies rejection sampling and supervised fine-tuning for distillation.
raw_results.answer: DeepSeek-R1 trains reasoning capabilities through a multi-stage learning framework that integrates reinforcement learning (RL). Specifically, the model is initially trained on a large dataset without any chain-of-thought annotations. During the training, DeepSeek-R1-Zero is subjected to RL training, where it is required to produce a reasoning process followed by the final answer. The RL training is designed to incentivize the model to develop advanced reasoning patterns such as self-reflection, verification, and dynamic strategy adaptation. This approach allows DeepSeek-R1 to learn reasoning strategies through reinforcement learning without the need for human-annotated reasoning data. The exact details of the RL training process and its effectiveness are detailed in Section 2 of the provided document [Source: DeepSeek_R1.pdf | Section: 2.3. Incentivize Reasoning Capability in LLMs | p.4].

raw_results.question: What architectural choices does MobileLLM make to optimize language models for mobile devices?
raw_results.ground_truth: MobileLLM prioritizes model depth over width, uses embedding sharing and grouped query attention, and applies immediate block-wise weight sharing to improve parameter efficiency for sub-billion parameter language models.
raw_results.answer:
MobileLLM optimizes language models for mobile devices through several architectural choices:
- Adopting deep and thin architectures to reduce model complexity while maintaining performance.
- Utilizing embedding sharing mechanisms to minimize the number of parameters.
- Implementing grouped-query attention mechanisms to improve computational efficiency.
- Proposing an immediate block-wise weight-sharing approach that reduces model size and only marginally increases latency overhead [Source: MobileLLM.pdf | Section: 2. Improving Sub-billion Scale LLM Design | p.2 | score: 0.843]. These choices enable MobileLLM to achieve notable accuracy boosts over existing models, demonstrating its effectiveness for on-device use cases [Source: MobileLLM.pdf | Section: for On-Device Use Cases | p.1 | score: 0.892].

raw_results.question: How does RT-2 transfer web-scale knowledge to robot manipulation?
raw_results.answer: RT-2 transfers web-scale knowledge to robot manipulation by leveraging pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Specifically, RT-2-PaLI-X, a specific instantiation of RT-2, combines physical skills learned from robot data with the ability to interpret images and text commands learned from web data. This transfer enables the model to deploy learned skills in new ways, demonstrating emergent capabilities such as re-purposing pick and place skills learned from robot data to place objects near semantically indicated locations [Source: RT-2_VLA.pdf | Section: 2. Can we observe and measure any emergent capabilities of RT-2? | p.7].

remarkable capabilities, combining the physical motions learned from the robot data with the ability

of dramatically improving generalization to novel objects and semantically varied instructions, we
observe a number of emergent capabilities. While the modelтАЩs physical skills are still limited to the
distribution of skills seen in the robot data, the model acquires the ability to deploy those skills in
new ways by interpreting images and language commands using knowledge gleaned from the web.

5. Limitations
Even though RT-2 exhibits promising generalization properties, there are multiple limitations of this
approach. First, although we show that including web-scale pretraining via VLMs boosts generalization
over semantic and visual concepts, the robot does not acquire any ability to perform new motions
by virtue of including this additional experience. The modelтАЩs physical skills are still limited to the
distribution of skills seen in the robot data (see Appendix G), but it learns to deploy those skills in
new ways. We believe this is a result of the dataset not being varied enough along the axes of skills.
An exciting direction for future work is to study how new skills could be acquired through new data
collection paradigms such as videos of humans.

raw_results.question: What problem does GraphRAG solve that naive RAG cannot handle?
raw_results.answer: GraphRAG addresses the limitation of naive RAG in handling iterative question answering that characterizes sensemaking activity, offering a highly efficient method despite a modest drop in performance compared to other global methods [Source: GraphRAG.pdf | Section: 2.2 Using Knowledge Graphs with LLMs and RAG | p.3]. Naive RAG may struggle with the iterative nature of sensemaking, where the system needs to continuously refine its understanding based on previous responses, whereas GraphRAG is designed to handle this complexity efficiently. However, the exact performance metrics and comparisons with naive RAG are not detailed in the provided documents.

GraphRAG builds upon prior work on advanced RAG strategies. GraphRAG leverages summaries
over large sections of the data source as a form of тАЭself-memoryтАЭ (described in Cheng et al. 2024),
which are later used to answer queries as in Mao et al. 2020). These summaries are generated in
parallel and iteratively aggregated into global summaries, similar to prior techniques (Feng et al.,
2023; Gao et al., 2023; Khattab et al., 2022; Shao et al., 2023; Su et al., 2020; Trivedi et al., 2022;
Wang et al., 2024). In particular, GraphRAG is similar to other approaches that use hierarchical
indexing to create summaries (similar to Kim et al. 2023; Sarthi et al. 2024). GraphRAG contrasts
with these approaches by generating a graph index from the source data, then applying graph-based
community detection to create a thematic partitioning of the data.
2.2
Using Knowledge Graphs with LLMs and RAG
Approaches to knowledge graph extraction from natural language text corpora include rule-

explicit techniques such as rewriting, decomposition, and disambiguation.
тАв HyDE (Gao et al., 2022): This method utilizes the LLM to generate a hypothetical document based
on the input query. This generated document is then employed to retrieve relevant text chunks,
which are subsequently used to formulate the final answer.
тАв GraphRAG (Edge et al., 2024): This is a graph-enhanced RAG system that utilizes an LLM to
extract entities and relationships from the text, representing them as nodes and edges. It generates
corresponding descriptions for these elements, aggregates nodes into communities, and produces a
community report to capture global information. When handling high-level queries, GraphRAG
retrieves more comprehensive information by traversing these communities.

of the LLM-as-a-judge technique (Zheng et al., 2024) suitable for questions targeting broad issues
and themes where there is no ground-truth answer. This approach first uses one LLM to generate
a diverse set of global sensemaking questions based on corpus-specific use cases, before using a
second LLM to judge the answers of two different RAG systems using predefined criteria (defined

real-world text datasets. Results show GraphRAG strongly outperforms vector RAG when using
